chain_of_thought: Native stack only shows the JNI bridge Java_com_ndrive_libmi9_liblicensing_LibLicensingMi9_parseLicense plus harness frames; intermediate native frames and the exact termination reason are unknown (shown as ??).

Library map indicates a single native library libmi9.so exporting the JNI method; however, we are not given any internal native stack frame or address to analyze, so we cannot identify a specific function below allocators/sanitizers as required.

Without a concrete crashing frame, Ghidra MCP cannot be meaningfully applied to trace memcpy/memmove or other memory operations, nor can we perform backward data-flow from a failing instruction or function.

Similarly, no Java callgraph details are provided beyond the JNI bridge name; we cannot see how Java constructs arguments or whether attacker-controlled data flows into parseLicense in the real app versus the fuzz harness.

The fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input strongly suggests that arbitrary data is being passed into parseLicense, but that is expected for fuzzing and does not, by itself, prove a real-world vulnerability unless we can see unsafe native handling of that data.

Given the missing stack and code context, we cannot demonstrate specific unsafe behavior such as out-of-bounds access, use-after-free, or unchecked memcpy attributable to attacker-controlled input; at best, we know the function can crash under fuzzing, which may also be due to harness misuse or deliberate assertions.

Because there is no concrete evidence of a specific bug pattern or exploitable condition and too many key details are absent, the most defensible classification is that a real vulnerability is not established from this crash alone.
